**Comparing textometric approaches: Reinert-style Descending Hierarchical Classification versus probabilistic and neural topic modeling on a web-sourced corpus of French rap lyrics.**

Robin Quillivic1, Jules Dilé-Toustou2 , Pierre Ratinaud3 

1 CESSP, Institut des Systèmes Compléxes, Paris Île-de-France, CNRS – [quillivicrobin@hotmail.fr](mailto:quillivicrobin@hotmail.fr)    
2 Université de Toulouse \- LERASS – [dile.jules@gmail.com](mailto:dile.jules@gmail.com)   
3 Université de Toulouse \- LERASS – [pierre.ratinaud@univ-tlse2.fr](mailto:pierre.ratinaud@univ-tlse2.fr) 

## **Abstract**

A genre in its own right, rap has dominated the French music industry since the 2000s and now enjoys unprecedented popularity. Caught between legitimization and stigmatization, French-language rap is becoming increasingly institutionalized, partly through public policy initiatives (Hammou, 2022). It is also gaining autonomy in economic and media terms, through growing market share, the emergence of independent labels, specialized journalists, and dedicated media platforms. Yet while this musical genre was originally rooted in social and political expression, it may now be undergoing a process of "mainstreaming" (variétisation), characterized by a thematic diversification driven by commercial interests (de Courson, 2024). Drawing on a corpus of rap lyrics from the Genius website, Benoît de Courson conducted lexicometric and topic modeling analyses on over 37,000 French-language rap tracks released between 1992 and 2024\. He identified seven topics and opened up several avenues of research that we aim to extend. First, the corpus is particularly complex, featuring extensive use of slang, verlan (French backslang), and various forms of textual noise, which call for dedicated linguistic preprocessing. Second, the seven identified topics may obscure certain thematic nuances, as the author himself acknowledges.  
Our proposal involves conducting a systematic comparative analysis of this same corpus using three complementary approaches: Descending Hierarchical Classification (DHC) via Iramuteq (Ratinaud, 2014), probabilistic topic modeling with LDA, and neural topic modeling with BERTopic. The comparison draws on multiple dimensions, including inter-model agreement, thematic distribution across artists, temporal dynamics, vocabulary overlap, and intra-topic coherence. Through this work, we aim not only to identify finer-grained themes and better characterize the evolution of French-language rap but also to contribute methodologically to the growing literature on comparing topic modeling approaches.   
**Keywords:**  Topic modeling; French rap; Textometry; Comparative analysis; BERTopic; LDA; Iramuteq

## **Résumé** 

## Genre à part, le rap submerge l'industrie musicale française depuis les années 2000 et connaît désormais une notoriété sans précédent. Entre légitimation et stigmatisation, le rap francophone s'institutionnalise, notamment à travers l'action des pouvoirs publics (Hammou, 2022). Il tend à s'autonomiser sur le plan économique et médiatique (en parts de marché, par la création de labels indépendants, de journalistes spécialisés et de médias dédiés). Mais si ce genre musical est initialement empreint de revendications sociales et politiques, il serait désormais sujet à un phénomène de variétisation, soit une diversification des thématiques à vocation commerciale (de Courson, 2024). Grâce à la constitution d'un corpus de textes de rap issus du site Genius, Benoît de Courson propose des analyses lexicométriques et de topic modeling sur plus de 37 000 morceaux de rap francophone parus entre 1992 et 2024\. Il identifie ainsi 7 topics et ouvre plusieurs pistes de recherche que nous souhaitons prolonger. En premier lieu, il s'agit d'un corpus complexe, largement constitué de mots d'argot, de verlan et de bruits divers, ce qui motive un traitement linguistique dédié. Ensuite, les 7 topics identifiés masquent potentiellement certaines spécificités thématiques, comme le souligne d'ailleurs l'auteur.

## Notre proposition consiste à mener une analyse comparative systématique de ce même corpus selon trois approches complémentaires : une Classification Hiérarchique Descendante (CHD) via Iramuteq (Ratinaud, 2014), un topic modeling probabiliste par LDA et un topic modeling neuronal par BERTopic. La comparaison s'appuie sur plusieurs dimensions, notamment l'accord inter-modèles, la distribution thématique selon les artistes, les dynamiques temporelles, le recouvrement lexical et la cohérence intra-topic. À travers ce travail, nous souhaitons non seulement identifier des thématiques plus fines et mieux caractériser l'évolution du rap francophone, mais également contribuer méthodologiquement à la littérature croissante portant sur la comparaison des approches de topic modeling.

**Mots-clés :**  Rap français ; Analyse comparative ; BERTopic ; LDA ; Iramuteq ; Distance de Labbé

## **1\. Introduction**

Originally a protest genre that emerged from the United States within the hip-hop culture, rap has gradually gained popularity in France since the 1990s. It is now recognized for both its cultural and economic significance, in terms of market share as well as by public institutions [(Hammou et Sonnette-Manouguian 2022\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XYwntC). However, its spread is also the result of contributions from a wide range of artists from diverse backgrounds. This lends it a heterogeneity that seems to be constantly growing, as evidenced by the emergence of new subgenres (emodrill, cloud rap, frapcore, plugg music, jersey, hyperpop, etc). To explore this diversity and identify its main trends, Benoît de Courson compiled a collection of 37,307 rap lyrics from the Genius website [(De Courson 2024\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8DS91q), a collaborative platform that catalogs song lyrics from around the world, similarly to Wikipedia. By offering a fresh perspective on French rap, his work identifies seven topics, using Bunka [(Dampierre et al. 2024\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?4Iv2Ss). This suggests a shift toward a "mainstreaming" (*variétisation*) following a period characterized by a more aggressive and vulgar style. The complexity of this analysis stems from the fact that rap lyrics constitute a rich but difficult-to-analyze corpus, composed in huge part of slang, french back slang (*verlan*), neologisms, and “ad-libs”. Our goal here is to continue this work by refining these results, based on the hypothesis that rap is more subject to a process of diversification than one *variétisation* and to determine which textometric method best captures the lexical richness of this corpus.  
Comparing topic modeling approaches has a long history. In the textometric tradition Lebart in [(Iezzi et al. 2020\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?0sSYUD), applied six methods, including rotated factor analysis, correspondence analysis, NMF, and LDA, to Shakespeare's 154 Sonnets, finding that most real topics were detected by multiple methods, though the overall configuration was, in his words, "not that good," and that exploratory tools remained essential to assess results. [(Alboni et al. 2023\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?3lQxpb) extended this cross-paradigm comparison to five methods (LDA, LSA, NMF, Lexical Correspondence Analysis, and Reinert's ALCESTE) on an e-mobility newsletter. Using Cramér's V to measure inter-method association, they found moderate agreement overall (V \= 0.52–0.68), but with a notable result: Reinert's method had the lowest overlap with all other methods and with the expert classification. In a complementary direction, [(Lelu et Cadot 2021\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?VxBGpB) systematically combined multiple clustering algorithms with multiple dataspace transformations across three contrasted English corpora, demonstrating, through 600 runs, that optimal method–dataspace combinations are highly corpus-dependent. In the NLP tradition,  [(Egger et Yu 2022\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fwcYGb) compared LDA, NMF, Top2Vec, and BERTopic on Twitter data, relying primarily on coherence scores and qualitative interpretation. Toolkits such as OCTIS  [(Terragni et al. 2021\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?s9sFw3) have since standardized the evaluation pipeline, but their metrics remain narrow, typically limited to topic coherence (C\_v, C\_NPMI) and diversity. More recently, [(Pereira et al. 2025\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?GMUTCO) argued that such word-based metrics fail to capture whether topics induce meaningful document groupings and proposed incorporating clustering metrics into the evaluation.  
Yet several fundamental challenges persist. First, a critical validation gap undermines the dominant evaluation paradigm: [(Hoyle et al. 2021\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jHmZQs) demonstrated that automated coherence metrics, developed for classical models, do not reliably predict human judgments when applied to neural topic models; automated evaluations declared a winning model when human evaluations did not. This calls into question the widespread practice of ranking models solely on coherence scores, which characterizes most BERTopic-vs-LDA studies [(Egger et Yu 2022; Gan et al., 2024\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?WUlLmQ). Second, existing comparisons, from Lebart's literary sonnets to Alboni et al.'s structured news items and to Cadot and Lelu's benchmark corpora, focus exclusively on well-structured, English-language text. Highly informal, non-standard registers such as French rap lyrics pose challenges that remain entirely unaddressed. Third, while [(Alboni et al. 2023\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NQDaMj) demonstrated the value of including Reinert's method alongside factorial and probabilistic approaches, no comparison framework has yet brought together the three major paradigms now available to researchers: Reinert's lexical classification, probabilistic modeling (LDA), and neural embedding-based clustering (BERTopic, like). Fourth, existing frameworks evaluate topic models through intrinsic quality metrics (coherence, diversity) and pairwise agreement (Cramér's V, as in Alboni et al.), but do not test whether topics capture known external structure (such as artist identity or temporal evolution) nor whether partitioning structures the full lexical space beyond the top-K representative words.  
Our work addresses these gaps by proposing a multi-dimensional comparison framework applied to a large corpus of French rap lyrics (115,805 verses, 605 artists, 1992–2023), systematically comparing Reinert's DHC via Iramuteq, LDA, and BERTopic across four dimensions. All code that can be reused in other contexts is available here: [https://github.com/psycholinguistics2125/JADT\_rap\_fr](https://github.com/psycholinguistics2125/JADT_rap_fr) .

## **2\. Corpus and Methods**

### ***2.1. Corpus***

The corpus is a subpart of de Courson's (2024) collection, consisting of lyrics scraped from the Genius platform. It comprises 115,805 individual verses (couplets) from 605 unique artists, spanning 1992 to 2023\. The unit of analysis is the verse rather than the full song, preserving intra-song thematic variation. The corpus is temporally skewed: the median year is 2017, with 52.6% of documents from the 2010s and 26.6% from the 2020s. The most prolific artist is JuL (2,878 verses). Preprocessing was performed with spaCy's French model for tokenization and lemmatization, with specific handling of slang and verlan using stop words and ruled based approach.

### ***2.2. Models*** 

We compare three paradigms, each with K=20 topics to enable fair comparison, a refinement of de Courson’s 7 topics, chosen to capture finer-grained thematic distinctions. 

*The Reinert method in IRaMuTeQ.* The results labeled "Iramuteq" in the remainder of this article were obtained by applying the Reinert method [(Ratinaud et Marchand 2012; Reinert 1983\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?5eAl5P) to the corpus indexed in IRaMuTeQ (Ratinaud, 2024, [« IRaMuTeQ »](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?psjHUk) ). The analysis, therefore, focuses on the lemmas of content words (nouns, adjectives, adverbs, verbs, and forms not present in the dictionary) with a frequency of 3 or greater (n=54,256). To facilitate comparison with other methods, the corpus is not segmented and uses the 120,662 couplets resulting from pre-processing as the units of analysis. It should be recalled that the classification is produced through a series of Correspondence Analyses (CA) performed on presence/absence matrices, where the units (in this case, song couplets) are crossed in rows with the selected content word lemmas in columns. The first factor of the CA is used to identify the bipartition of the matrix rows that maximizes the extracted information. At each step, the algorithm proceeds with the largest generated matrix and stops once it has reached the pre-set number of classes. For this analysis, we requested 400 classes to obtain 20 terminal classes that meet the default minimum-size criterion (i.e., the number of analyzed units divided by the number of requested classes). These 20 terminal classes retain 115,805 units (95.97% of the corpus). These specific texts are then used as input for the other methods.

*LDA.* Latent Dirichlet Allocation [(Blei et al.](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?EWnKnP), 2003\) is a generative probabilistic model that represents documents as mixtures of topics, each topic being a distribution over words. We used Gensim's implementation with bigram preprocessing, symmetric α, automatic η, 15 passes, and 400 iterations, minimum document frequency of 5, and maximum ratio of 0.3. Multiple parameters were tested, but not presented in this article

*BERTopic.* BERTopic [(Grootendorst 2022\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?u4Nf5Q) uses transformer embeddings (all-mpnet-base-v2) for document representation, UMAP for dimensionality reduction (15 neighbors, 5 components, cosine metric), and KMeans clustering. Topics are represented via class-based c-TF-IDF weighting.

### ***2.3. Evaluation metrics***

We evaluate models across four dimensions, going beyond the coherence-and-diversity paradigm dominant in the NLP literature [(Terragni et al. 2021; Hoyle et al. 2021\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?xxmCEU). Dimensions 3 and 4 are, to our knowledge, absent from standard topic model comparison frameworks.

*D1 \- Inter-model agreement.*  We measure whether the three models produce similar partitions using two complementary metrics: the Adjusted Rand Index [(Hubert et Arabie 1985\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?16LtDg) , which counts pairwise agreement between clusterings corrected for chance (range \[-1, 1\], where 0 is random), and Normalized Mutual Information [(Strehl et Ghosh, 2002\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?zt3ND5), which quantifies shared information between partitions normalized by entropy (range \[0, 1\], where 0 is independence).

*D2 \- Metadata sensitivity*. We test whether topics capture known external structure, specifically artist identity, using Cramér's V  (Cramér, 1946\) on the artist × topic contingency table.

*D3 \- Intra-topic homogeneity and inter-topic separation.* We compute pairwise distances between documents within the same topic (intra) and between each topic and the rest of the corpus (inter), using two complementary metrics: the Labbé distance [(Labbé 2007\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?WNSNPr), a standard in French textometry and stylometry, and the Jensen-Shannon divergence (Lin, 1991). Because the Labbé distance is sensitive to text length [(Cortelazzo et al. 2013\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?mkLoxh), and individual verses average only \~68 lemmatized tokens, we compute distances on aggregated pseudo-documents: for each topic, n verses are randomly concatenated into larger textual units. We report results for three aggregation sizes (n \= 8, 20, and 72 verses) to show that the ranking between models is stable across scales. We derive the Separation Ratio (SR \= mean inter-topic distance / mean intra-topic distance); values above 1 indicate that topics are more distinct from each other than they are internally heterogeneous. We note that SR serves a similar function to the silhouette score commonly used in cluster evaluation, but operates on raw word distributions shared by all models rather than in a model-specific embedding space, making it a fairer basis for cross-paradigm comparison.

#### *D4 \- Global lexical structuration.* We compute χ²/n on the full word × topic contingency table built from the presence/absence matrix, where N is the total number of word-form occurrences. Unlike coherence (which evaluates only the top-K words per topic) or Labbé/JS (which evaluate document pairs), χ²/n captures how strongly the entire partition structures the full lexical space.

## **3\. Results**

### ***3.1. 	I*****nter-model agreement (D1)**

All pairwise agreements between models are very low, indicating that each paradigm captures genuinely distinct structure in the data.

Table X. Intermodel agreement metrics

| Pair | ARI | NMI | Interpretation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| BERTopic vs LDA | 0.008 | 0.015 | Near-random |
| BERTopic vs Iramuteq | 0.044 | 0.102 | Weak agreement	 |
| LDA vs Iramuteq | 0.005 | 0.015 | Near-random |

The best, though still modest, agreement is between BERTopic and Iramuteq (NMI \= 0.102). BERTopic–LDA and LDA–Iramuteq are both near-random (NMI ≈ 0.015). This contrasts with the moderate agreement found by Alboni et al. (2023) on structured news (Cramér's V \= 0.52–0.68) and the partial convergence reported by Lebart (2020) on literary text, suggesting that inter-model divergence increases with corpus informality and complexity.

### ***3.2. Artist topic association***

Table X. Artist–topic association (Cramér's V).

| Model | Cramér's V |
| :---- | :---- |
| Iramuteq | 0.385 |
| BERTopic | 0.208 |
| LDA | 0.114 |

Iramuteq produces the strongest association between artists and topics (V \= 0.385), with 12.2% of artists classified as specialists (\>50% of their verses in a single topic). This is consistent with Reinert's design: the method identifies distinct lexical worlds, which, in this corpus, correspond partly to artist-specific vocabularies. BERTopic shows moderate association (V \= 0.208), while LDA distributes artists more evenly across topics (V \= 0.114), suggesting it captures broader co-occurrence patterns rather than artist-level signatures.

### ***3.3. Cluster quality (D3)***

We report intra-topic and inter-topic Labbé distances for three aggregation sizes. As pseudo-documents grow longer, absolute Labbé distances decrease monotonically (short texts have higher sampling variance), but the Separation Ratio remains stable or increases, confirming that the ranking between models is robust and structural.

Table X. Labbé distances by aggregation size and SR ratio.

| Model | N aggregation \= 8 |  |  | N aggregation \= 20 |  |  | N aggregation \= 72 |  |  |
| :---- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
|  | **Intra** | **Inter** | **SR** | **Intra** | **Inter** | **SR** | **Intra** | **Inter** | **SR** |
| Iramuteq | 0.854 | 0.891 | 1.043 | 0.754 | 0.813 | 1.078 | 0.588 | 0.688 | 1.170 |
| BERTopic | 0.862 | 0.881 | 1.022 | 0.766 | 0.794 | 1.037 | 0.603 | 0.653 | 1.083 |
| LDA | 0.863 | 0.875 | 1.014 | 0.766 | 0.784 | 1.024 | 0.601 | 0.630 | 1.048 |

Three observations emerge. First, Iramuteq achieves the highest Separation Ratio across all aggregation sizes. Second, the ranking Iramuteq \> BERTopic \> LDA is perfectly stable across all three scales. Third, the SR increases with aggregation size, from 1.043 to 1.170 for Iramuteq, meaning that the lexical distinctiveness of Iramuteq’s classes becomes more apparent as sampling noise is reduced. This growing gap confirms that the differences are structural, not artifactual. The Jensen-Shannon divergence yields the same ranking (see the code report).

**Figure 1\.** Labbé distance stabilization by aggregation size. Left: intra-topic (homogeneity). Right: inter-topic (separation). Shaded areas show standard deviation across topics.

![][image1]

Figure 1 illustrates the smooth, monotonic decrease of Labbé distances with aggregation size for all models. Crucially, the vertical gap between inter- and intra-topic curves (i.e., the numerator of SR minus its denominator) widens with aggregation for Iramuteq, remains moderate for BERTopic, and is smallest for LDA. Iramuteq also exhibits the largest standard deviation across topics, reflecting the heterogeneity of its classes: some capture tight artist-specific lexicons, others broader thematic registers.

### ***3.4. Global lexical structuration (D4)***

The χ²/n metric measures how strongly each model’s partition structures the full vocabulary. We compute it on the word-form × topic contingency table built from Iramuteq’s presence/absence matrix (5,257,462 total occurrences).

Table X. Intermodel agreement metrics

| Model | χ² | N | χ²/n |
| :---- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Iramuteq | 1,143,624 | 5,257,462 | 0.218 |
| BERTopic (mpnet) | 1,075,395 | 5,257,462 | 0.205 |
| LDA (bigram) | 1,020,161 | 5,257,462 | 0.194 |

Iramuteq’s partition produces a χ²/n of 0.218, ahead of BERTopic (0.205) and LDA (0.194). Each token in the corpus deviates more from the lexical-independence baseline when assigned by Iramuteq than by the other models. Varying BERTopic’s embedding model (CamemBERT: 0.203, multilingual-e5: 0.210) and LDA’s n-gram settings (trigram: 0.201, both: 0.197) shows sensitivity to configuration, but the ranking Iramuteq \> BERTopic \> LDA holds across all tested variants.

## **4\. Discussions**

### ***4.1. The evolution of French Rap***

Based on Iramuteq analysis we observe a radical shift since 2014, as de Courson pointed out (2024). However, this method allows us to identify a more complex phenomenon.  The 20 Iramuteq classes cluster into 4 macro-groups corresponding to distinct stylistic registers. The first part provides a clearer definition of the so-called "vulgar" category, as well as certain artists associated with it (such as the Neochrome collective and Sexion d'Assault). This category accounted for a proportionally significant share of the content until around 2018\.  A second distinct group of songs emerged in 2014\. It is characterized by the influence of the prolific rapper Jul, known for his atypic phrasing, and the emergence of ad-libs typical of “trap music” and later “drill”. At the same time, four other categories emerge, consisting of slang terms and neologisms associated with illegality, trafficking, and marginal lifestyles. These, too, are markers specific to the emergence of the “drill” style. Another distinct category consists of names and acronyms associated with, among others, the rappers of the 667 collective. A third category includes poetic, romantic, and even philosophical lexical forms. It is the only one that has persisted over the years. A fourth group has declined significantly. It consists of a category associated with the old-school style of the 1990s and 2000s, which has almost completely disappeared. The share of “conscious rap” remains minimally significant, as does a category focused more on sinister and dark vocabulary. Even less significant, a category consisting of English tool-words persists over time despite its small part. Despite the proportional decline in explicitly politicized rap, these analyses offer a broader interpretation of the categories of French-language rap. The gradual emergence of new subgenres suggests a diversification of styles, some of which echo hip-hop's origins as a marginal culture grappling with issues of violence and drugs, yet managing to break free through music. Others blend with influences from Afrobeat, pop, or rock.

![][image2]

**Figure 2\.** Dendogram of IRamuTeq Classsfication

![][image3]

**Figure 3\.** Temporal evolution

### ***4.2. Methodological implications***

The near-zero inter-model agreement (NMI \< 0.11) is itself an informative finding rather than a failure. It means the three paradigms are not interchangeable: Iramuteq captures lexical worlds organized by distinctive vocabulary, BERTopic groups documents by semantic proximity in embedding space, and LDA models word co-occurrence distributions. This extends the gradient observed in the literature: from partial convergence on small literary corpora (Lebart, 2020), to moderate agreement on structured news [(Alboni et al. 2023\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?i1DRet) (Alboni et al., 2023), to near-independence on a large informal corpus,  suggesting that divergence might increases with corpus complexity and register heterogeneity.

Iramuteq’s consistent superiority on all lexical metrics is partly by design: Reinert’s method explicitly maximizes χ² between classes, directly optimizing lexical structuration. But beyond this structural advantage, Iramuteq offers several practical strengths that the current literature tends to overlook. It is fully transparent: the classification relies on Correspondence Analysis and χ² tests, both well-understood statistical tools, and the resulting classes are directly interpretable through their characteristic vocabulary ranked by χ² test-values. It is computationally efficient: the entire 115,805-document analysis runs in minutes on a standard laptop, with no GPU requirement. It is reproducible: the double-classification procedure (retaining only documents consistently assigned across two independent runs) provides a built-in stability check that neither LDA nor BERTopic offers natively. And it naturally produces a hierarchical structure (the dendrogram), enabling researchers to explore topics at varying levels of granularity without re-running the analysis.

By contrast, the claims of BERTopic’s superiority found in recent literature [(Egger et Yu 2022; Gan et al. 2024\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?FXRtRW) rest on narrow empirical grounds. These comparisons typically rely on coherence scores, which [(Hoyle et al. 2021\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?bQZs5J) showed do not reliably predict human judgments for neural models , applied to well-structured English corpora where BERTopic’s pre-trained embeddings are in their comfort zone. On our corpus of informal French text, BERTopic uses English-language embeddings (all-mpnet-base-v2), which are unlikely to capture the semantic nuances of verlan, argot, and artist-specific neologisms. Furthermore, BERTopic’s pipeline involves multiple stochastic components (UMAP projection, clustering initialization) whose interaction makes results sensitive to random seeds, while requiring substantially more computational resources. Its topics, characterized by c-TF-IDF weights on top of opaque embedding clusters, are less directly interpretable than Iramuteq’s χ²-ranked vocabulary profiles.  This does not make BERTopic valueless. It captures cross-cutting semantic groupings (topics that share vocabulary because they are semantically related) that Iramuteq’s sharp lexical boundaries, by design, miss. For exploratory work where semantic proximity matters more than lexical distinctiveness, BERTopic provides a complementary perspective. LDA, for its part, produces the smoothest but least informative partition, evenly distributing artists and vocabulary across topics, which may suit applications that require stable probabilistic baselines. The choice between methods should be guided by the research question and corpus characteristics, not by claims of universal superiority based on a single metric evaluated on a single type of corpus.

We propose χ²/n on the word × topic contingency table as a practical corpus-level metric for comparing how strongly different models structure a given corpus. Its interpretation is straightforward: higher values mean stronger lexical contrast between topics. The absolute value depends on vocabulary size and K, so it is meaningful only for comparisons on the same corpus with the same K.

### ***4.3. Future work***

This study opens up several avenues for further research. First, from a methodological perspective, the comparison of textometric analyses warrants further investigation. We suggest applying these methods to new corpora, perhaps less heterogeneous ones, such as corpora of news articles. This suggests adapting the code for any corpus comparison.

Then, from a perspective more focused on rap music, we would like to complete these analyses : (i) Use artist metadata about biography, (ii) In-depth analysis of rap corpus through a report. We’ll analyze the subgenres to examine their specific vocabulary and themes. Another interesting research topic is to continue identifying markers of sexism and compare their use over time and across artists and subgenres.

### ***4.4. Limitation***

In this exercise, we tested the BERT and LDA models using a corpus that had been partially reduced by the Iramuteq analysis. In other words, the methods did not work on strictly identical corpora (4% less text for BERT and LDA), even though all comparisons were based on exactly the same texts (all calculations were performed on the 115,805 units retained in the classification). We should emphasize that this practice simplifies comparisons by allowing evaluation metrics to be calculated on identical sets; furthermore, it is entirely representative of a standard application of the Reinert method algorithm, which tends to identify and eliminate small sets of units that are nearly identical to each other yet highly distinct from the rest of the corpus. For the LDA model, which assigns a probability of topic membership to each text and selects a single topic based on the highest probability, even for short texts, this approach likely represents a disadvantage. The decision not to segment the texts forces Iramuteq to work on units averaging 160 tokens, whereas the default settings typically use segments of 40 tokens. This reduces the precision of the analysis. In the same vein, the number of classes selected for this experiment (20)—while also chosen to simplify comparisons—is not, from an analytical standpoint, ideally suited to this corpus. The resulting large classes still conceal a significant amount of heterogeneity. Finally, it must be noted that it would have been preferable if one of the developers of the methods under review had not participated in the comparison process.

## **References**

[Alboni, Fabrizio, Pasquale Pavone, et Margherita Russo. 2023\. « The Search for Topics Related to Electric Mobility: A Comparative Analysis of Some of the Most Widely Used Methods in the Literature ». *METRON* 81 (3): 367‑91.](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?DUoLGN)   
[Blei, David M., Andrew Y. Ng, et Michael I. Jordan. 2003\. « Latent Dirichlet Allocation ». *Journal of Machine Learning Research* 3 (Jan): 993‑1022.](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?DUoLGN)   
[Cortelazzo, Michele, Paolo Nadalutti, et Arjuna Tuzzi. 2013\. « Improving Labbé’s Intertextual Distance: Testing a Revised Version on a Large Corpus of Italian Literature ». *Journal of Quantitative Linguistics* 20 (mai). https://doi.org/10.1080/09296174.2013.773138.](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?DUoLGN)   
[Dampierre, Charles de, Andrei Mogoutov, et Nicolas Baumard. 2024\. « Towards Transparency: Exploring LLM Trainings Datasets through Visual Topic Modeling and Semantic Frame ». arXiv:2406.06574.
